He is considered a good [[:en:God|God]] and patron of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], since the Japanese population has for millennia lived off the bounty of the sea. Ryūjin is also credited with the challenge of a hurricane which sank the Mongolian flotilla sent by [[:en:Kublai Khan|Kublai Khan]].{{Citation needed|reason=Your explanation here|date=November 2020}} Ryūjin lived in [[Ryūgū-jō]], his palace under the sea built out of red and white [[:en:coral|coral]], from where he controlled the [[:en:Tide|Tide]]s with magical [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]]. [[:en:Sea turtle|Sea turtle]]s, [[:en:Fish|Fish]], [[:en:Jellyfish|Jellyfish]], [[:en:Snake|Snake]]s, and other sea creatures are often seen as Ryūjin's servants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gagne|first=Tammy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|title=Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date=2018-12-15|publisher=ABDO|isbn=978-1-5321-7070-6|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042748/https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042746/https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Mythology==
=== How the jellyfish lost its bones === One legend involving Ryūjin is the story about how the [[:en:Jellyfish|Jellyfish]] lost its [[:en:Bone|Bone]]s. According to this story, Ryūjin wanted to eat [[:en:Monkey|monkey's]] [[:en:Liver|Liver]] (in some versions of the story, to heal an incurable [[:en:rash|rash]]), and sent the jellyfish to get him a monkey. The monkey managed to sneak away from the jellyfish by telling him that he had put his liver in a jar in the forest and offered to go and get it. As the jellyfish came back and told Ryūjin what had happened, Ryūjin became so angry that he beat the jellyfish until its bones were crushed.
=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant [[:en:Centipede|Centipede]] attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>
=== Empress Jingū === According to [[:en:en:Legend|en:Legend]], the Empress [[:en:Jingu of Japan|Jingū]] was able to carry out her attack into [[:en:Korea|Korea]] with the help of Ryūjin's [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]]. Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean [[:en:Navy|Navy]], Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] [[:en:Goddess|Goddess]] who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081203140730/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=795 Ryūjin shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto *[https://libmma.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/collection/p15324coll10/id/90981 Netsuke: masterpieces from the Metropolitan Museum of Art], an exhibition catalog from The Metropolitan Museum of Art (fully available online as PDF), which contains many representations of Ryūjin {{commonscatinline|Ryūjin}} {{jmyth navbox long}}

'''Ryūjin''' (, {{Lit|Dragon God}}), which in some traditions is equivalent to '''[[:en:Watatsumi|Ōwatatsumi]]''', was the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of the [[:en:Sea|Sea]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. In many versions Ryūjin had the ability to transform into a [[:en:Human|Human]] shape. Many believed the god had knowledge on medicine and many considered him as the bringer of rain and thunder, Ryūjin is also the patron god (''[[ujigami]]'') of several family groups.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Ph.D|first1=Evans Lansing Smith|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|title=The Complete Idiot's Guide to World Mythology|last2=Brown|first2=Nathan Robert|date=2008-07-01|publisher=Penguin|isbn=978-1-101-04716-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114326/https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Ryujin|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|access-date=2020-11-13|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|archive-date=2021-04-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419021748/https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|url-status=live}}</ref>
This [[:en:Japanese dragon|Japanese dragon]], symbolizing the power of the [[:en:Ocean|Ocean]], had a large mouth. He is considered a good [[:en:God|God]] and patron of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], since the Japanese population has for millennia lived off the bounty of the sea. Ryūjin is also credited with the challenge of a hurricane which sank the Mongolian flotilla sent by [[:en:Kublai Khan|Kublai Khan]].{{Citation needed|reason=Your explanation here|date=November 2020}} Ryūjin lived in [[Ryūgū-jō]], his palace under the sea built out of red and white [[:en:coral|coral]], from where he controlled the [[:en:Tide|Tide]]s with magical [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]]. [[:en:Sea turtle|Sea turtle]]s, [[:en:Fish|Fish]], [[:en:Jellyfish|Jellyfish]], [[:en:Snake|Snake]]s, and other sea creatures are often seen as Ryūjin's servants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gagne|first=Tammy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|title=Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date=2018-12-15|publisher=ABDO|isbn=978-1-5321-7070-6|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042748/https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042746/https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Mythology==
=== How the jellyfish lost its bones === One legend involving Ryūjin is the story about how the [[:en:Jellyfish|Jellyfish]] lost its [[:en:Bone|Bone]]s. According to this story, Ryūjin wanted to eat [[:en:Monkey|monkey's]] [[:en:Liver|Liver]] (in some versions of the story, to heal an incurable [[:en:rash|rash]]), and sent the jellyfish to get him a monkey. The monkey managed to sneak away from the jellyfish by telling him that he had put his liver in a jar in the forest and offered to go and get it. As the jellyfish came back and told Ryūjin what had happened, Ryūjin became so angry that he beat the jellyfish until its bones were crushed.
=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant [[:en:Centipede|Centipede]] attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>
=== Empress Jingū === According to [[:en:en:Legend|en:Legend]], the Empress [[:en:Jingu of Japan|Jingū]] was able to carry out her attack into [[:en:Korea|Korea]] with the help of Ryūjin's [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]]. Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean [[:en:Navy|Navy]], Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] [[:en:Goddess|Goddess]] who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />

File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>

=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant [[:en:Centipede|Centipede]] attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>
=== Empress Jingū === According to [[:en:en:Legend|en:Legend]], the Empress [[:en:Jingu of Japan|Jingū]] was able to carry out her attack into [[:en:Korea|Korea]] with the help of Ryūjin's [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]]. Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean [[:en:Navy|Navy]], Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] [[:en:Goddess|Goddess]] who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[sn:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] {{Short description|Japanese deity of the sea}} {{about|the Japanese deity||Ryujin (disambiguation)}} {{More citations needed|date=December 2019}}{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto Romaji | name = Ryūjin | image = Tamatori being pursued bya dragon.jpg | caption = [[Princess Tamatori]] steals Ryūjin's jewel, by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]. }}
'''Ryūjin''' (, {{Lit|Dragon God}}), which in some traditions is equivalent to '''[[:en:Watatsumi|Ōwatatsumi]]''', was the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of the [[:en:Sea|Sea]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. In many versions Ryūjin had the ability to transform into a [[:en:Human|Human]] shape. Many believed the god had knowledge on medicine and many considered him as the bringer of rain and thunder, Ryūjin is also the patron god (''[[ujigami]]'') of several family groups.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Ph.D|first1=Evans Lansing Smith|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|title=The Complete Idiot's Guide to World Mythology|last2=Brown|first2=Nathan Robert|date=2008-07-01|publisher=Penguin|isbn=978-1-101-04716-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114326/https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Ryujin|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|access-date=2020-11-13|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|archive-date=2021-04-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419021748/https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|url-status=live}}</ref>
This [[:en:Japanese dragon|Japanese dragon]], symbolizing the power of the [[:en:Ocean|Ocean]], had a large mouth. He is considered a good [[:en:God|God]] and patron of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], since the Japanese population has for millennia lived off the bounty of the sea. Ryūjin is also credited with the challenge of a hurricane which sank the Mongolian flotilla sent by [[:en:Kublai Khan|Kublai Khan]].{{Citation needed|reason=Your explanation here|date=November 2020}} Ryūjin lived in [[Ryūgū-jō]], his palace under the sea built out of red and white [[:en:coral|coral]], from where he controlled the [[:en:Tide|Tide]]s with magical [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]]. [[:en:Sea turtle|Sea turtle]]s, [[:en:Fish|Fish]], [[:en:Jellyfish|Jellyfish]], [[:en:Snake|Snake]]s, and other sea creatures are often seen as Ryūjin's servants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gagne|first=Tammy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|title=Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date=2018-12-15|publisher=ABDO|isbn=978-1-5321-7070-6|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042748/https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042746/https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Mythology==
=== How the jellyfish lost its bones === One legend involving Ryūjin is the story about how the [[:en:Jellyfish|Jellyfish]] lost its [[:en:Bone|Bone]]s. According to this story, Ryūjin wanted to eat [[:en:Monkey|monkey's]] [[:en:Liver|Liver]] (in some versions of the story, to heal an incurable [[:en:rash|rash]]), and sent the jellyfish to get him a monkey. The monkey managed to sneak away from the jellyfish by telling him that he had put his liver in a jar in the forest and offered to go and get it. As the jellyfish came back and told Ryūjin what had happened, Ryūjin became so angry that he beat the jellyfish until its bones were crushed.
=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant [[:en:Centipede|Centipede]] attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>
=== Empress Jingū === According to [[:en:en:Legend|en:Legend]], the Empress [[:en:Jingu of Japan|Jingū]] was able to carry out her attack into [[:en:Korea|Korea]] with the help of Ryūjin's [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]]. Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean [[:en:Navy|Navy]], Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] [[:en:Goddess|Goddess]] who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081203140730/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=795 Ryūjin shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto *[https://libmma.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/collection/p15324coll10/id/90981 Netsuke: masterpieces from the Metropolitan Museum of Art], an exhibition catalog from The Metropolitan Museum of Art (fully available online as PDF), which contains many representations of Ryūjin {{commonscatinline|Ryūjin}} {{jmyth navbox long}}

The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[commons:" to "[[co:commons:"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[sn:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] {{Short description|Japanese deity of the sea}} {{about|the Japanese deity||Ryujin (disambiguation)}} {{More citations needed|date=December 2019}}{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto Romaji | name = Ryūjin | image = Tamatori being pursued bya dragon.jpg | caption = [[Princess Tamatori]] steals Ryūjin's jewel, by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]. }}
'''Ryūjin''' (, {{Lit|Dragon God}}), which in some traditions is equivalent to '''[[:en:Watatsumi|Ōwatatsumi]]''', was the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of the [[:en:Sea|Sea]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. In many versions Ryūjin had the ability to transform into a [[:en:Human|Human]] shape. Many believed the god had knowledge on medicine and many considered him as the bringer of rain and thunder, Ryūjin is also the patron god (''[[ujigami]]'') of several family groups.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Ph.D|first1=Evans Lansing Smith|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|title=The Complete Idiot's Guide to World Mythology|last2=Brown|first2=Nathan Robert|date=2008-07-01|publisher=Penguin|isbn=978-1-101-04716-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114326/https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Ryujin|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|access-date=2020-11-13|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|archive-date=2021-04-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419021748/https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|url-status=live}}</ref>
This [[:en:Japanese dragon|Japanese dragon]], symbolizing the power of the [[:en:Ocean|Ocean]], had a large mouth. He is considered a good [[:en:God|God]] and patron of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], since the Japanese population has for millennia lived off the bounty of the sea. Ryūjin is also credited with the challenge of a hurricane which sank the Mongolian flotilla sent by [[:en:Kublai Khan|Kublai Khan]].{{Citation needed|reason=Your explanation here|date=November 2020}} Ryūjin lived in [[Ryūgū-jō]], his palace under the sea built out of red and white [[:en:coral|coral]], from where he controlled the [[:en:Tide|Tide]]s with magical [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]]. [[:en:Sea turtle|Sea turtle]]s, [[:en:Fish|Fish]], [[:en:Jellyfish|Jellyfish]], [[:en:Snake|Snake]]s, and other sea creatures are often seen as Ryūjin's servants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gagne|first=Tammy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|title=Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date=2018-12-15|publisher=ABDO|isbn=978-1-5321-7070-6|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042748/https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042746/https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Mythology==
=== How the jellyfish lost its bones === One legend involving Ryūjin is the story about how the [[:en:Jellyfish|Jellyfish]] lost its [[:en:Bone|Bone]]s. According to this story, Ryūjin wanted to eat [[:en:Monkey|monkey's]] [[:en:Liver|Liver]] (in some versions of the story, to heal an incurable [[:en:rash|rash]]), and sent the jellyfish to get him a monkey. The monkey managed to sneak away from the jellyfish by telling him that he had put his liver in a jar in the forest and offered to go and get it. As the jellyfish came back and told Ryūjin what had happened, Ryūjin became so angry that he beat the jellyfish until its bones were crushed.
=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant [[:en:Centipede|Centipede]] attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>
=== Empress Jingū === According to [[:en:en:Legend|en:Legend]], the Empress [[:en:Jingu of Japan|Jingū]] was able to carry out her attack into [[:en:Korea|Korea]] with the help of Ryūjin's [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]]. Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean [[:en:Navy|Navy]], Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] [[:en:Goddess|Goddess]] who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081203140730/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=795 Ryūjin shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto *[https://libmma.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/collection/p15324coll10/id/90981 Netsuke: masterpieces from the Metropolitan Museum of Art], an exhibition catalog from The Metropolitan Museum of Art (fully available online as PDF), which contains many representations of Ryūjin {{commonscatinline|Ryūjin}} {{jmyth navbox long}}

'''Ryūjin''' (, {{Lit|Dragon God}}), which in some traditions is equivalent to '''{{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Ōwatatsumi}}''', was the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} of the {{ill|Sea|en|Sea|WD=Q165|lt=Sea}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. In many versions Ryūjin had the ability to transform into a {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human}} shape. Many believed the god had knowledge on medicine and many considered him as the bringer of rain and thunder, Ryūjin is also the patron god (''[[ujigami]]'') of several family groups.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Ph.D|first1=Evans Lansing Smith|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|title=The Complete Idiot's Guide to World Mythology|last2=Brown|first2=Nathan Robert|date=2008-07-01|publisher=Penguin|isbn=978-1-101-04716-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114326/https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Ryujin|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|access-date=2020-11-13|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|archive-date=2021-04-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419021748/https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|url-status=live}}</ref>
This {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|WD=Q2366503|lt=Japanese dragon}}, symbolizing the power of the {{ill|Ocean|en|Ocean|WD=Q9430|lt=Ocean}}, had a large mouth. He is considered a good {{ill|God|en|God|WD=Q190|lt=God}} and patron of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, since the Japanese population has for millennia lived off the bounty of the sea. Ryūjin is also credited with the challenge of a hurricane which sank the Mongolian flotilla sent by {{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|WD=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan}}.{{Citation needed|reason=Your explanation here|date=November 2020}} Ryūjin lived in [[Ryūgū-jō]], his palace under the sea built out of red and white {{ill|coral|en|coral|WD=Q171446|lt=coral}}, from where he controlled the {{ill|Tide|en|Tide|WD=Q23384|lt=Tide}}s with magical {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|WD=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels}}. {{ill|Sea turtle|en|Sea turtle|WD=Q219329|lt=Sea turtle}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish}}, {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}}s, and other sea creatures are often seen as Ryūjin's servants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gagne|first=Tammy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|title=Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date=2018-12-15|publisher=ABDO|isbn=978-1-5321-7070-6|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042748/https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042746/https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Mythology==
=== How the jellyfish lost its bones === One legend involving Ryūjin is the story about how the {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish}} lost its {{ill|Bone|en|Bone|WD=Q265868|lt=Bone}}s. According to this story, Ryūjin wanted to eat {{ill|Monkey|en|Monkey|WD=Q1367|lt=monkey's}} {{ill|Liver|en|Liver|WD=Q9368|lt=Liver}} (in some versions of the story, to heal an incurable {{ill|rash|en|rash|WD=Q653197|lt=rash}}), and sent the jellyfish to get him a monkey. The monkey managed to sneak away from the jellyfish by telling him that he had put his liver in a jar in the forest and offered to go and get it. As the jellyfish came back and told Ryūjin what had happened, Ryūjin became so angry that he beat the jellyfish until its bones were crushed.
=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant {{ill|Centipede|en|Centipede|WD=Q43447|lt=Centipede}} attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>

Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean {{ill|Navy|en|Navy|WD=Q4508|lt=Navy}}, Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess}} who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />

File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[sn:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] {{Short description|Japanese deity of the sea}} {{about|the Japanese deity||Ryujin (disambiguation)}} {{More citations needed|date=December 2019}}{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto Romaji | name = Ryūjin | image = Tamatori being pursued bya dragon.jpg | caption = [[Princess Tamatori]] steals Ryūjin's jewel, by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]. }}
'''Ryūjin''' (, {{Lit|Dragon God}}), which in some traditions is equivalent to '''{{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Ōwatatsumi}}''', was the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} of the {{ill|Sea|en|Sea|WD=Q165|lt=Sea}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. In many versions Ryūjin had the ability to transform into a {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human}} shape. Many believed the god had knowledge on medicine and many considered him as the bringer of rain and thunder, Ryūjin is also the patron god (''[[ujigami]]'') of several family groups.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Ph.D|first1=Evans Lansing Smith|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|title=The Complete Idiot's Guide to World Mythology|last2=Brown|first2=Nathan Robert|date=2008-07-01|publisher=Penguin|isbn=978-1-101-04716-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114326/https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Ryujin|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|access-date=2020-11-13|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|archive-date=2021-04-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419021748/https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|url-status=live}}</ref>
This {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|WD=Q2366503|lt=Japanese dragon}}, symbolizing the power of the {{ill|Ocean|en|Ocean|WD=Q9430|lt=Ocean}}, had a large mouth. He is considered a good {{ill|God|en|God|WD=Q190|lt=God}} and patron of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, since the Japanese population has for millennia lived off the bounty of the sea. Ryūjin is also credited with the challenge of a hurricane which sank the Mongolian flotilla sent by {{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|WD=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan}}.{{Citation needed|reason=Your explanation here|date=November 2020}} Ryūjin lived in [[Ryūgū-jō]], his palace under the sea built out of red and white {{ill|coral|en|coral|WD=Q171446|lt=coral}}, from where he controlled the {{ill|Tide|en|Tide|WD=Q23384|lt=Tide}}s with magical {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|WD=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels}}. {{ill|Sea turtle|en|Sea turtle|WD=Q219329|lt=Sea turtle}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish}}, {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}}s, and other sea creatures are often seen as Ryūjin's servants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gagne|first=Tammy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|title=Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date=2018-12-15|publisher=ABDO|isbn=978-1-5321-7070-6|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042748/https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042746/https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Mythology==
=== How the jellyfish lost its bones === One legend involving Ryūjin is the story about how the {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish}} lost its {{ill|Bone|en|Bone|WD=Q265868|lt=Bone}}s. According to this story, Ryūjin wanted to eat {{ill|Monkey|en|Monkey|WD=Q1367|lt=monkey's}} {{ill|Liver|en|Liver|WD=Q9368|lt=Liver}} (in some versions of the story, to heal an incurable {{ill|rash|en|rash|WD=Q653197|lt=rash}}), and sent the jellyfish to get him a monkey. The monkey managed to sneak away from the jellyfish by telling him that he had put his liver in a jar in the forest and offered to go and get it. As the jellyfish came back and told Ryūjin what had happened, Ryūjin became so angry that he beat the jellyfish until its bones were crushed.
=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant {{ill|Centipede|en|Centipede|WD=Q43447|lt=Centipede}} attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>
=== Empress Jingū === According to {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend}}, the Empress [[:en:Jingu of Japan|Jingū]] was able to carry out her attack into {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} with the help of Ryūjin's {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|WD=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels}}. Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean {{ill|Navy|en|Navy|WD=Q4508|lt=Navy}}, Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess}} who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081203140730/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=795 Ryūjin shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto *[https://libmma.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/collection/p15324coll10/id/90981 Netsuke: masterpieces from the Metropolitan Museum of Art], an exhibition catalog from The Metropolitan Museum of Art (fully available online as PDF), which contains many representations of Ryūjin {{commonscatinline|Ryūjin}} {{jmyth navbox long}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[sn:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] {{Short description|Japanese deity of the sea}} {{about|the Japanese deity||Ryujin (disambiguation)}} {{More citations needed|date=December 2019}}{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto Romaji | name = Ryūjin | image = Tamatori being pursued bya dragon.jpg | caption = [[Princess Tamatori]] steals Ryūjin's jewel, by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]. }}
'''Ryūjin''' (, {{Lit|Dragon God}}), which in some traditions is equivalent to '''{{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Ōwatatsumi}}''', was the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} of the {{ill|Sea|en|Sea|WD=Q165|lt=Sea}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. In many versions Ryūjin had the ability to transform into a {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human}} shape. Many believed the god had knowledge on medicine and many considered him as the bringer of rain and thunder, Ryūjin is also the patron god (''[[ujigami]]'') of several family groups.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Ph.D|first1=Evans Lansing Smith|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|title=The Complete Idiot's Guide to World Mythology|last2=Brown|first2=Nathan Robert|date=2008-07-01|publisher=Penguin|isbn=978-1-101-04716-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114326/https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Ryujin|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|access-date=2020-11-13|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|archive-date=2021-04-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419021748/https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|url-status=live}}</ref>
This {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|WD=Q2366503|lt=Japanese dragon}}, symbolizing the power of the {{ill|Ocean|en|Ocean|WD=Q9430|lt=Ocean}}, had a large mouth. He is considered a good {{ill|God|en|God|WD=Q190|lt=God}} and patron of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, since the Japanese population has for millennia lived off the bounty of the sea. Ryūjin is also credited with the challenge of a hurricane which sank the Mongolian flotilla sent by {{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|WD=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan}}.{{Citation needed|reason=Your explanation here|date=November 2020}} Ryūjin lived in [[Ryūgū-jō]], his palace under the sea built out of red and white {{ill|coral|en|coral|WD=Q171446|lt=coral}}, from where he controlled the {{ill|Tide|en|Tide|WD=Q23384|lt=Tide}}s with magical {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|WD=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels}}. {{ill|Sea turtle|en|Sea turtle|WD=Q219329|lt=Sea turtle}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish}}, {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}}s, and other sea creatures are often seen as Ryūjin's servants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gagne|first=Tammy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|title=Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date=2018-12-15|publisher=ABDO|isbn=978-1-5321-7070-6|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042748/https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042746/https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Mythology==
=== How the jellyfish lost its bones === One legend involving Ryūjin is the story about how the {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish}} lost its {{ill|Bone|en|Bone|WD=Q265868|lt=Bone}}s. According to this story, Ryūjin wanted to eat {{ill|Monkey|en|Monkey|WD=Q1367|lt=monkey's}} {{ill|Liver|en|Liver|WD=Q9368|lt=Liver}} (in some versions of the story, to heal an incurable {{ill|rash|en|rash|WD=Q653197|lt=rash}}), and sent the jellyfish to get him a monkey. The monkey managed to sneak away from the jellyfish by telling him that he had put his liver in a jar in the forest and offered to go and get it. As the jellyfish came back and told Ryūjin what had happened, Ryūjin became so angry that he beat the jellyfish until its bones were crushed.
=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant {{ill|Centipede|en|Centipede|WD=Q43447|lt=Centipede}} attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>
=== Empress Jingū === According to {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend}}, the Empress [[:en:Jingu of Japan|Jingū]] was able to carry out her attack into {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} with the help of Ryūjin's {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|WD=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels}}. Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean {{ill|Navy|en|Navy|WD=Q4508|lt=Navy}}, Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess}} who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081203140730/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=795 Ryūjin shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto *[https://libmma.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/collection/p15324coll10/id/90981 Netsuke: masterpieces from the Metropolitan Museum of Art], an exhibition catalog from The Metropolitan Museum of Art (fully available online as PDF), which contains many representations of Ryūjin {{commonscatinline|Ryūjin}} {{jmyth navbox long}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[sn:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] {{Short description|Japanese deity of the sea}} {{about|the Japanese deity||Ryujin (disambiguation)}} {{More citations needed|date=December 2019}}{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto Romaji | name = Ryūjin | image = Tamatori being pursued bya dragon.jpg | caption = [[Princess Tamatori]] steals Ryūjin's jewel, by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]. }}
'''Ryūjin''' (, {{Lit|Dragon God}}), which in some traditions is equivalent to '''{{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Ōwatatsumi}}''', was the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity}} of the {{ill|Sea|en|Sea|WD=Q165|lt=Sea}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. In many versions Ryūjin had the ability to transform into a {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human}} shape. Many believed the god had knowledge on medicine and many considered him as the bringer of rain and thunder, Ryūjin is also the patron god (''[[ujigami]]'') of several family groups.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Ph.D|first1=Evans Lansing Smith|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|title=The Complete Idiot's Guide to World Mythology|last2=Brown|first2=Nathan Robert|date=2008-07-01|publisher=Penguin|isbn=978-1-101-04716-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114326/https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Ryujin|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|access-date=2020-11-13|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|archive-date=2021-04-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419021748/https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|url-status=live}}</ref>
This {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|WD=Q2366503|lt=Japanese dragon}}, symbolizing the power of the {{ill|Ocean|en|Ocean|WD=Q9430|lt=Ocean}}, had a large mouth. He is considered a good {{ill|God|en|God|WD=Q190|lt=God}} and patron of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, since the Japanese population has for millennia lived off the bounty of the sea. Ryūjin is also credited with the challenge of a hurricane which sank the Mongolian flotilla sent by {{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|WD=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan}}.{{Citation needed|reason=Your explanation here|date=November 2020}} Ryūjin lived in [[Ryūgū-jō]], his palace under the sea built out of red and white {{ill|coral|en|coral|WD=Q171446|lt=coral}}, from where he controlled the {{ill|Tide|en|Tide|WD=Q23384|lt=Tide}}s with magical {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|WD=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels}}. {{ill|Sea turtle|en|Sea turtle|WD=Q219329|lt=Sea turtle}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish}}, {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}}s, and other sea creatures are often seen as Ryūjin's servants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gagne|first=Tammy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|title=Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date=2018-12-15|publisher=ABDO|isbn=978-1-5321-7070-6|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042748/https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042746/https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Mythology==
=== How the jellyfish lost its bones === One legend involving Ryūjin is the story about how the {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish}} lost its {{ill|Bone|en|Bone|WD=Q265868|lt=Bone}}s. According to this story, Ryūjin wanted to eat {{ill|Monkey|en|Monkey|WD=Q1367|lt=monkey's}} {{ill|Liver|en|Liver|WD=Q9368|lt=Liver}} (in some versions of the story, to heal an incurable {{ill|rash|en|rash|WD=Q653197|lt=rash}}), and sent the jellyfish to get him a monkey. The monkey managed to sneak away from the jellyfish by telling him that he had put his liver in a jar in the forest and offered to go and get it. As the jellyfish came back and told Ryūjin what had happened, Ryūjin became so angry that he beat the jellyfish until its bones were crushed.
=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant {{ill|Centipede|en|Centipede|WD=Q43447|lt=Centipede}} attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>
=== Empress Jingū === According to {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend}}, the Empress [[:en:Jingu of Japan|Jingū]] was able to carry out her attack into {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} with the help of Ryūjin's {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|WD=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels}}. Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean {{ill|Navy|en|Navy|WD=Q4508|lt=Navy}}, Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess}} who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081203140730/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=795 Ryūjin shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto *[https://libmma.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/collection/p15324coll10/id/90981 Netsuke: masterpieces from the Metropolitan Museum of Art], an exhibition catalog from The Metropolitan Museum of Art (fully available online as PDF), which contains many representations of Ryūjin {{commonscatinline|Ryūjin}} {{jmyth navbox long}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[co:commons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:Рюджин]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ryūjin]][[de:Ryūjin]][[en:Ryūjin]][[eo:Riuĝin]][[es:Ryūjin]][[eu:Ryūjin]][[fa:ریوجین]][[fr:Ryūjin]][[id:Ryūjin]][[it:Ryūjin]][[mni:ꯔ꯭ꯌꯨꯖꯤꯟ]][[ru:Рюдзин]][[simple:Ryūjin]][[sv:Ryujin]][[te:ర్యూజిన్]][[th:รีวจิง]][[uk:Рюдзін]][[vi:Ryūjin]] {{Short description|Japanese deity of the sea}} {{about|the Japanese deity||Ryujin (disambiguation)}} {{More citations needed|date=December 2019}}{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto Romaji | name = Ryūjin | image = Tamatori being pursued bya dragon.jpg | caption = [[Princess Tamatori]] steals Ryūjin's jewel, by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]. }}
'''Ryūjin''' (, {{Lit|Dragon God}}), which in some traditions is equivalent to '''{{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Ōwatatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}''', was the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} of the {{ill|Sea|en|Sea|WD=Q165|lt=Sea|1=Sea}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. In many versions Ryūjin had the ability to transform into a {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human|1=Human}} shape. Many believed the god had knowledge on medicine and many considered him as the bringer of rain and thunder, Ryūjin is also the patron god (''[[ujigami]]'') of several family groups.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Ph.D|first1=Evans Lansing Smith|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|title=The Complete Idiot's Guide to World Mythology|last2=Brown|first2=Nathan Robert|date=2008-07-01|publisher=Penguin|isbn=978-1-101-04716-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114326/https://books.google.com/books?id=UYdGl8XM8L0C&q=ryujin+mythology&pg=PA231|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Ryujin|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|access-date=2020-11-13|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|archive-date=2021-04-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419021748/https://www.worldhistory.org/Ryujin/|url-status=live}}</ref>
This {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|WD=Q2366503|lt=Japanese dragon|1=Japanese dragon}}, symbolizing the power of the {{ill|Ocean|en|Ocean|WD=Q9430|lt=Ocean|1=Ocean}}, had a large mouth. He is considered a good {{ill|God|en|God|WD=Q190|lt=God|1=God}} and patron of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, since the Japanese population has for millennia lived off the bounty of the sea. Ryūjin is also credited with the challenge of a hurricane which sank the Mongolian flotilla sent by {{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|WD=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan|1=Kublai Khan}}.{{Citation needed|reason=Your explanation here|date=November 2020}} Ryūjin lived in [[Ryūgū-jō]], his palace under the sea built out of red and white {{ill|coral|en|coral|WD=Q171446|lt=coral|1=Coral}}, from where he controlled the {{ill|Tide|en|Tide|WD=Q23384|lt=Tide|1=Tide}}s with magical {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|WD=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels|1=Tide jewels}}. {{ill|Sea turtle|en|Sea turtle|WD=Q219329|lt=Sea turtle|1=Sea turtle}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish|1=Fish}}, {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish|1=Jellyfish}}, {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake|1=Snake}}s, and other sea creatures are often seen as Ryūjin's servants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gagne|first=Tammy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|title=Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date=2018-12-15|publisher=ABDO|isbn=978-1-5321-7070-6|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042748/https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-10-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211008042746/https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Mythology==
=== How the jellyfish lost its bones === One legend involving Ryūjin is the story about how the {{ill|Jellyfish|en|Jellyfish|WD=Q30178|lt=Jellyfish|1=Jellyfish}} lost its {{ill|Bone|en|Bone|WD=Q265868|lt=Bone|1=Bone}}s. According to this story, Ryūjin wanted to eat {{ill|Monkey|en|Monkey|WD=Q1367|lt=monkey's|1=Monkey}} {{ill|Liver|en|Liver|WD=Q9368|lt=Liver|1=Liver}} (in some versions of the story, to heal an incurable {{ill|rash|en|rash|WD=Q653197|lt=rash|1=Rash}}), and sent the jellyfish to get him a monkey. The monkey managed to sneak away from the jellyfish by telling him that he had put his liver in a jar in the forest and offered to go and get it. As the jellyfish came back and told Ryūjin what had happened, Ryūjin became so angry that he beat the jellyfish until its bones were crushed.
=== The Tale of Tawara Tōda === {{Further information|Tawara Tōda Monogatari}} One myth involves Ryūjin asking a man by the name of Tawara Tōda to help him get rid of a giant {{ill|Centipede|en|Centipede|WD=Q43447|lt=Centipede|1=Centipede}} attacking his kingdom. Tawara Tōda agrees to help Ryūjin and Tawara Tōda accompanies Ryūjin back to his home. When Tawara Tōda killed the centipede, Ryūjin awarded him with a bag of rice.<ref>{{Cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=ry%C5%ABjin|isbn = 9781576074671|title = Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year = 2003|publisher = ABC-CLIO|access-date = 2020-11-13|archive-date = 2022-06-10|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220610123247/https://www.google.com/books/edition/Handbook_of_Japanese_Mythology/gqs-y9R2AekC?hl=en&gbpv=1&bsq=ry%C5%ABjin|url-status = live}}</ref>
=== Empress Jingū === According to {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend|1=Legend}}, the Empress [[:en:Jingu of Japan|Jingū]] was able to carry out her attack into {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} with the help of Ryūjin's {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|WD=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels|1=Tide jewels}}. Some versions of the legend say that Empress Jingū asked [[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]] to go down to Ryūjin's palace and retrieve the tide jewels.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|access-date=2020-12-16|archive-date=2021-12-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214114324/https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ryujin|url-status=live}}</ref>
Upon confronting the Korean {{ill|Navy|en|Navy|WD=Q4508|lt=Navy|1=Navy}}, Jingū threw the {{nihongo|''kanju''|干珠|"tide-ebbing jewel"}} into the sea, and the tide receded. The Korean fleet was stranded, and the men got out of their ships. Jingū then threw down the {{nihongo|''manju''|満珠|"tide-flowing jewel"}} and the water rose, drowning the Korean soldiers.
== Family == Ryūjin was the father of the beautiful [[:en:Toyotama-Hime|Toyotama-Hime]] {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess|1=Goddess}} who married the hunter prince [[Hoori]]. The first {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, is said to have been a grandson of Otohime and Hoori's. Thus, Ryūjin is said to be one of the ancestors of the Japanese imperial dynasty.
==Worship== {{nihongo|''Ryūjin shinkō''|竜神信仰|"dragon god faith"}} is a form of [[Shinto]] religious belief that worships dragons as water ''[[kami]]''. It is connected with agricultural rituals, rain prayers, and the success of fishermen.
The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081203140730/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=795 Ryūjin shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto *[https://libmma.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/collection/p15324coll10/id/90981 Netsuke: masterpieces from the Metropolitan Museum of Art], an exhibition catalog from The Metropolitan Museum of Art (fully available online as PDF), which contains many representations of Ryūjin {{commonscatinline|Ryūjin}} {{jmyth navbox long}}

The god has shrines across Japan and especially in rural areas where fishing and rains for agriculture are important for local communities.<ref name=":0" />
==In art== <gallery caption="Ryūjin or Ryūo in art"> File:Japan Takenouchi no Sukune Meets the Dragon King of the Sea img02 DMA.jpg|The Dragon King part of a statue representing "Takenouchi no Sukune Meeting the Dragon King of the Sea", dated 1875–1879, Japan. File:Dragon King, view 2, 11th to early 12 century, Japan, wood with traces of pigment - Art Institute of Chicago - DSC00134.JPG|Dragon King sculpture with residual traces of pigment, dated 11th–12th century, Japan. </gallery>
